469	        protected internal virtual async Task OnBlurredAsync(FocusEventArgs obj)
470	        {
471	            _isFocused = false;
472	
473	            if (ReadOnly)
474	            {
475	                return;
476	            }
477	
478	            // all the OnBlur parents (TextField, MudMask, NumericField, DateRange, etc) currently point to this method
479	            // which causes this method to be fired repeatedly, we can use the obj.Type of FocusedEventArgs to track it
480	
481	            if (!OnlyValidateIfDirty || _isDirty)
482	            {
483	                Touched = true;
484	                if (_validated)
485	                {
486	                    if (OnBlur.HasDelegate)
487	                    {
488	                        obj.Type += ".additional";
489	                        await OnBlur.InvokeAsync(obj);
490	                    }
491	                }
492	                else
493	                {
494	                    if (OnBlur.HasDelegate)
495	                    {
496	                        obj.Type += ".additional";
497	                        await BeginValidationAfterAsync(OnBlur.InvokeAsync(obj));
498	                    }
499	                    else
500	                    {
501	                        await ValidateValue();
502	                    }
503	                }
504	            }
505	        }

521	        protected virtual async Task SetValueAndUpdateTextAsync(T? value, bool updateText = true, bool force = false)
522	        {
523	            var valueChanged = !EqualityComparer<T?>.Default.Equals(ReadValue, value);
524	
525	            if (!valueChanged && !force)
526	            {
527	                return;
528	            }
529	
530	            _isDirty = true;
531	            _validated = false;
532	
533	            // Use ParameterState to set Value instead of direct assignment
534	            // This ensures proper parameter lifecycle management
535	            await _valueState.SetValueAsync(value);
536	
537	            // If force is true but value hasn't changed, ParameterState won't fire the callback
538	            // so we need to manually invoke it to maintain backward compatibility
539	            if (force && !valueChanged)
540	            {
541	                await ValueChanged.InvokeAsync(value);
542	            }
543	
544	            if (updateText)
545	            {
546	                await UpdateTextPropertyAsync(false);
547	            }
548	
549	            FieldChanged(value);
550	            await BeginValidateAsync();
551	        }

553	        private async Task OnValueParameterChangedAsync(ParameterChangedEventArgs<T?> arg)
554	        {
555	            _validated = false;
556	            var wasTouched = Touched;
557	
558	            // When Value changes from parent, update Text from Value
559	            // But only if Text is not also being set in the same parameter update
560	            // Check ParameterView to see if Text is also present
561	            if (!arg.ParameterView.Contains<string?>(nameof(Text)))
562	            {
563	                // Always update text when Value changes (TextUpdateSuppression removed)
564	                _forceTextUpdate = false;
565	                await UpdateTextPropertyAsync(false);
566	            }
567	
568	            // Notify the form that the field has changed and trigger re-validation
569	            // Only do this after the field has been touched.
570	            if (wasTouched)
571	            {
572	                FieldChanged(arg.Value);
573	                await BeginValidateAsync();
574	            }
575	        }

668	        /// <inheritdoc />
669	        public override async Task SetParametersAsync(ParameterView parameters)
670	        {
671	            var hasText = parameters.Contains<string>(nameof(Text));
672	            var hasValue = parameters.Contains<T>(nameof(Value));
673	            var currentValue = ReadValue;
674	            await base.SetParametersAsync(parameters);
675	
676	            // Refresh Text from Value if Value is present but Text is not
677	            // This maintains backward compatibility with the old `if (hasValue && !hasText)` logic
678	            // ParameterState only fires OnValueParameterChangedAsync when value CHANGES,
679	            // but we need to update Text even when Value is passed unchanged (for formatting)
680	            if (hasValue && !hasText)
681	            {
682	                var valueChanged = !EqualityComparer<T?>.Default.Equals(currentValue, ReadValue);
683	
684	                // Preserve in-progress user text across parent rerenders until Value actually changes.
685	                if (_isFocused && !valueChanged && !_forceTextUpdate)
686	                {
687	                    return;
688	                }
689	
690	                // Always update text when Value changes (TextUpdateSuppression removed)
691	                _forceTextUpdate = false;
692	                await UpdateTextPropertyAsync(false);
693	            }
694	        }

718	                // Because of this, we must manually call OnParametersSet on the ParameterContainer to ensure ParameterState fields update correctly.

720	                // Without this manual call, scenarios involving inherited components can fall out of sync. For example:
721	                // - Component1 inherits a base component that defines a state parameter.
722	                // - Component2 also inherits that same base component, wraps Component1, and forwards its base parameters to Component1.

But wait — the blur handling for `MudInput` works through `CallOnBlurredAsync()` which is triggered by JS. Let me check whether the `MudSelect` also sets up the JS blur handling on the `MudInput` inside it.

So when the `MudInput` loses focus via JS, `MudInput.CallOnBlurredAsync()` calls `this.OnBlurredAsync()`. But `this` is the `MudInput`, not `MudSelect`. The `OnBlurredAsync` in `MudBaseInput` is what sets `Touched = true` and triggers validation. 

But when the `MudInput` inside `MudSelect` blurs, it will call its own `OnBlurredAsync` — but does `MudSelect` override this method? Let me check.

content.text: I see that `CallOnBlurredAsync` is invoked via JS and is not directly callable from tests. Let me look at how other tests trigger validation on blur. Let me search for tests that do focus/blur testing:

Good, so the pattern for testing is to call `OnBlurredAsync(new FocusEventArgs())` on the component instance. Let me now look at how the test selects the MudInput inside the MudSelect:

Let me create a test. First, let me understand the exact issue better. Let me check which `ReadOnly` value the inner `MudInput` has:

Looking at the razor: `ReadOnly="true"` — oh! The inner `MudInput` is always `ReadOnly="true"`. And in `OnBlurredAsync`:
```csharp
if (ReadOnly) { return; }
```

So when the JS blur fires on the inner `MudInput`, `OnBlurredAsync` returns early because `ReadOnly` is true! So the inner `MudInput` never calls `ValidateValue()` either. But it also never fires the `OnBlur` event callback through the `OnBlurredAsync` path.

Wait, let me re-read `MudInput.CallOnBlurredAsync()`:
```csharp
[JSInvokable]
public async Task CallOnBlurredAsync()
{
    if (!_isFocused)
        return;
    await OnBlurredAsync(new FocusEventArgs { Type = "jsBlur.OnBlur" });
}
```

This calls `this.OnBlurredAsync()` on MudInput. The `OnBlurredAsync` in `MudBaseInput`:
```csharp
protected internal virtual async Task OnBlurredAsync(FocusEventArgs obj)
{
    _isFocused = false;
    if (ReadOnly) { return; }  // <-- ReadOnly is true for inner MudInput!
    ...
}
```

So `OnBlurredAsync` returns early because `ReadOnly = true`. This means the `OnBlur` event callback is never invoked either!

So the flow IS:
1. JS detects blur on inner MudInput HTML element
2. `MudInput.CallOnBlurredAsync()` is called
3. `OnBlurredAsync` checks `ReadOnly` → returns early
4. **No validation is triggered**
5. **The `OnBlur` event is never invoked**

This means the fix needs to handle this differently. The `OnBlur` event on the inner `MudInput` is never actually fired via the JS blur path because `ReadOnly` causes early return.

So the test I wrote doesn't actually reproduce the bug because it directly calls `OnBlurredAsync` on the `MudSelect`. The real scenario is that when the inner HTML input loses focus, the `MudSelect`'s `OnBlurredAsync` is never reached.

content.text:
The test still fails. The issue is that `CallOnBlurredAsync` on the inner `MudInput` doesn't trigger `OnFocusOutAsync` on the `MudSelect`. The `CallOnBlurredAsync` calls `OnBlurredAsync` which just sets `_isFocused = false` and returns because of `ReadOnly=true`. It doesn't bubble up to the `@onfocusout` on the outer div.

Let me think about a different approach. The test calls `innerInput.Instance.CallOnBlurredAsync()` which is the JS-invoked blur. But this blur is on the `MudInput` component, not the HTML element. Let me check if there's a way to trigger the actual `onfocusout` on the outer div from tests.

Actually, I think the right approach is different. Let me reconsider. The issue the user reports is with tabbing. In a real browser:

1. User tabs to the MudSelect's inner input element
2. User tabs away from the MudSelect
3. The browser fires `focusout` on the inner input element → bubbles to the outer div → `OnFocusOutAsync` fires

So the real question is: does the `focusout` on the inner HTML input bubble to the outer div? Yes, `focusout` bubbles. And the `@onfocusout` on the outer div catches it.

But in the test, when I call `CallOnBlurredAsync()` on the inner MudInput, this is a .NET method call — it doesn't trigger DOM `focusout`. Let me try a different approach: trigger the `focusout` on the DOM element.

        public async Task Select_Should_ShowValidationOnBlur()
        {
            // Arrange: create a required select with no value selected
            var comp = Context.Render<SelectRequiredTest>();

            var mudSelect = comp.FindComponent<MudSelect<string>>().Instance;

            // Initially, validation errors should be empty (Touched is false)
            mudSelect.ValidationErrors.Should().BeEmpty();

            // Act: Simulate focus leaving the select, which triggers OnFocusOutAsync
            // on the outer div. This is the real browser behavior when tabbing out.
            var outerDiv = comp.Find($"#{mudSelect.ElementId}");
            await outerDiv.FocusOutAsync(new FocusEventArgs());

            // Assert: Validation errors should appear after blur
            // The MudSelect should show "Required" error after focus loss
            mudSelect.ValidationErrors.Should().NotBeEmpty();
            mudSelect.ValidationErrors.First().Should().Be("Required");
        }
